coredata.dc:title: A novel sustainable biomass-based fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of salicylic acid in rice
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yongqiang
Li, Wenting
Hu, Xuetao
Zhang, Xinai
Huang, Xiaowei
Li, Zhihua
Zhai, Xiaodong
Shen, Tingting
Shi, Jiyong
He, Yong
Zou, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed for sensitive detection of salicylic acid (SA) in rice using silk-derived carbon quantum dots @ Curcumin @ iron-based metal organic framework (SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs). Fe-MOFs with porous structure not only provided holes for SCQDs to evade self-aggregation of SCQDs, but Fe2+ ions from MOFs was ingeniously employed to capture active sites of Cur, solving the problem of lacking sufficient specificity of Cur to SA while converting weak response signal to amplified “turn on” mode. Upon exposed to SA, the probe interacted with SA to form Cur-Fe2+-SA ternary complex, which inhibited the internal filtration effect between Cur and SCQDs, and triggered a cascade of response signaling. With this strategy, the proposed probe achieved sensitive determination of salicylic acid in rice with detection limit as low as 0.14 μmol/L. This study provides unique insight into constructing economical and eco-friendly fluorescent sensor for SA detection with superior performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salicylic acid
Fluorescent probe
CQDs
MOFs
Curcumin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of silk-based carbon quantum dots (SCQDs)
Determination of SA levels in rice samples
Characterization of morphology and chemical structures
Analytical performances of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe to SA
Determination of SA in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32071895 ). Project of Agricultural Equipment Department of Jiangsu University ( NZXB20200212 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of synthesis of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe; (b) TEM image of SCQDs, illustrated with high-resolution TEM of SCQDs; (c-d) SEM image of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs; (e) TEM image of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs; (f) the corresponding EDS spectrum of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs from Fig. 1(d) and (g) distribution of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O) and iron (Fe) on its surface.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of the prepared materials; (c) The full XPS spectrum of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs and the corresponding high resolution XPS spectrum for (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s and (f) O 1 s.

The dependence of fluorescent intensities of SCQDs on excitation wavelength and (b) the corresponding contour map; (c) Effect of Cur amount on the luminescent properties of SCQDs; (d) The emission spectra of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs before and after adding SA solution; (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized materials; (f) The measured Zeta potentials of the prepared materials.

(a) The calculated infrared and (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of target molecules. The Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) of (c) Cur and (d) Cur@Fe2+; The calculated electrostatic potential of (e) Cur and (f) Cur@Fe2+.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the prepared probe exposed to salicylic acid at different concentrations (0–200 μmol/L); (b) The dependence of probe response value on salicylic acid concentration; (c) The response value of the probe to 10 μmol/L salicylic acid and 100 μmol/L other potential interfering substances; (d) The relationship between the probe response value to salicylic acid detection and storage time (0–15 days).
Schematic diagram of the SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe for salicylic acid detection.
Performance comparison of several reported methods with the developed SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe for salicylic acid detection.

head.ce:title: A novel sustainable biomass-based fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of salicylic acid in rice

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed for sensitive detection of salicylic acid (SA) in rice using silk-derived carbon quantum dots @ Curcumin @ iron-based metal organic framework (SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs). Fe-MOFs with porous structure not only provided holes for SCQDs to evade self-aggregation of SCQDs, but Fe2+ ions from MOFs was ingeniously employed to capture active sites of Cur, solving the problem of lacking sufficient specificity of Cur to SA while converting weak response signal to amplified “turn on” mode. Upon exposed to SA, the probe interacted with SA to form Cur-Fe2+-SA ternary complex, which inhibited the internal filtration effect between Cur and SCQDs, and triggered a cascade of response signaling. With this strategy, the proposed probe achieved sensitive determination of salicylic acid in rice with detection limit as low as 0.14 μmol/L. This study provides unique insight into constructing economical and eco-friendly fluorescent sensor for SA detection with superior performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Salicylic acid (SA) is considered as an important phytohormone involved in the signaling and regulation of a variety of physiological processes in rice plant, such as seed germination, plant respiration and flowering (Chen, Feng, Zhou, Ji, Que, & Wang, 2019). Mutants with elevated endogenous SA levels has been well established to contribute to increasing the resistance of plants against biotic and abiotic stresses (Kulak, Jorrín-Novo, Romero-Rodriguez, Yildirim, Gul, & Karaman, 2021). Thus, the determination of salicylic acid level in rice can timely understand the health status of rice. SA has also been used as illegal food preservatives in food industry, e.g. some illegal traders illegally added headache powder (mainly composed of salicylic acid) to Chinese Baijiu to alleviate headaches and other discomfort caused by inferior Baijiu. However, the misuse and abuse of SA and its derivatives may cause serious allergic reaction of angioedema and asthma for susceptible population. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop rapid and accurate method for the quantification of SA to ensure food security and safety.

Traditional methods for SA detection include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Zheng, Yoo, Abd El-Aty, Park, Choi, Kim, et al., 2019), mass spectrometry (MS) or tandem MS coupled chromatography (Balcke, Handrick, Bergau, Fichtner, Henning, Stellmach, et al., 2012), and capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Chang, Huang, Fu, Wu, Wu, & Lin, 2017). However, these methods usually involve expensive and bulky instruments, complicated operating procedures, high solvent consumption or potentially hazardous to operators, leading to the high expense and low efficiency for SA detection, and driving the development of various phytohormone detection methods, such as electrochemical method (Zhang, Li, Li, Wang, Long, Li, et al., 2021), aptamer-based biosensors (Feng, Chen, Song, Ding, Wang, & Que, 2021), colorimetric assay (Liang, Huang, Liu, Zuo, Ye, & Zhao, 2020), Raman spectrometry (Adomavičiūtė, Velička, & Šablinskas, 2020) and fluorescence analysis (Chen, Yang, Tang, Wang, Dong, Wu, et al., 2020). Among them, fluorescence assay may be an attractive and promising alternative for the detection of SA since it offers substantial advantages over traditional methods in terms of fast response, low cost, environmental friendliness and competitive sensitivity (Li, Zhang, Hu, Shi, Liang, Huang, et al., 2022; Shi, Wu, Li, Lin, Qu, Shen, et al., 2022), and this technology has been widely used in the determination of target molecules (Li, Hu, Li, Shi, Zhai, Xu, et al., 2020).

To date, several fluorescent probes have been prepared and employed for SA determination, such as rhodamine (Wang, Luo, Yang, Zhao, Dong, Li, et al., 2019), lanthanide complexs (Meng, Bai, Da, Cui, Li, & Pan, 2019) and semiconductor quantum dots (Bunkoed & Kanatharana, 2015). However, these approaches come with intrinsic drawbacks and limitations. For examples, rhodamine is considered a potentially dangerous material, which can cause chronic poisoning and increase risk of carcinogenesis when exposed to human skin. The cost of lanthanide complexes is also relatively high due to the use of more expensive metal raw materials (Zhang, Zhong, Zhao, Shen, Li, & Liu, 2022). Therefore, given the concept of sustainable development and green chemistry, it may be more practical to prepare economical and environmentally-benign probes. More importantly, these reported probes with single-emission property were employed for detecting the targets, various environmental factors, e.g. the fluctuation of excitation source and background fluorescence, would inevitably affect the stability and reliability of the obtained results in the detection process (Liang, Hu, Li, Wang, Guo, Huang, et al., 2022). In contrast, ratiometric fluorescent probes with dual-emission features are endowed with the self-calibration capacity by taking the ratio of the two monitored fluorescence signals as the outputs, greatly improving the reliability of the approach (Hu, Guo, Wang, Liu, Yang, & Fang, 2022). Unfortunately, there have been few report on the preparation and application of ratiometric probe for SA detection.

Recently, some natural biomasses, derived from naturally available sources, are considered to be promising alternatives to traditional fluorophores due to their low or non-toxicity, easy availability and environmental compatibility. Particularly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared with biomasses (e.g. konjac flour (Teng, Ma, Ge, Yan, Yang, Zhang, et al., 2014), aloe vera (Devi, Saini, & Kim, 2019)) have attracted increasing interests of researchers in biosensing and biomedicine owing to their fascinating fluorescence properties, broad resources of raw materials and low cost. Besides, curcumin (Cur), a natural polyphenol extracted from turmeric, is usually used as safe spice and coloring agent in food industry, and it is also considered a promising fluorophore along with excellent photoluminescence property (Liu, Zhang, Pan, Li, Yu, & Liu, 2021). Previous studies have demonstrated that the diketone structure in Cur can combine with metals to form complexes (Kumar, Kaushik, Rani, Rafat, Shabir, Dev, et al., 2021) and can further incorporate SA molecule to form the ternary complex (Chen et al., 2020). However, Cur can couple many common metal ions, which means Cur will “face” the problem of lacking sufficient specificity for target detection. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are hybrid porous materials constructed by the chemical coordination of metal ions with organic ligands, and their advantages in large surface area and high porosity make them popular in various fields, such as gas adsorption, catalysis and sensing (Mohan, Kumar, Xi, Ma, Tao, Xing, et al., 2022). MOFs can not only provide cavities for introduced quantum dots to avoid self-aggregation but allow in situ enrichment of target analytes (Shi, Kong, Li, Wei, Wei, Qu, et al., 2023). Importantly, employing metal ions in MOFs to induce the capture of the active sites of Cur may be an effective strategy to solve the insufficient specificity of Cur. So far, there have been few works in previous literatures on using the strategy to construct fluorescent probes.

Herein, an environmentally-sound ratiometric fluorescent probe based on silk derived carbon quantum dots @ Curcumin @ iron based metal organic framework (SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs) was proposed for sensitive detection of SA. As depicted in Scheme 1 , the probe with dual fluorescence properties was prepared by hydrothermal method. In the probe, the fluorescence of Cur was quenched by Fe2+ due to the complexation between Cur and Fe-MOFs, and the inner filter effect (IFE) between SCQDs and Cur further resulted in the fluorescence quenching of SCQDs. Upon exposed to SA, under the induction of Fe2+ ions from MOFs, Cur@Fe2+ specifically captured SA and then formed a ternary complex with SA, which caused significant changes in the fluorescence signals of SCQDs and Cur. To our knowledge, this is the first report to employ MOFs as sensing mediator and utilize the competitive interactions between MOFs and natural fluorophores for SA detection. The working mechanism of the probe was thoroughly studied and discussed in this study.

Cocoons of silkworm were provided by Zhejiang textile Co., Ltd. (China), curcumin, iron(II) acetate anhydrous, 2,5-Dihydroxyterephthalic acid (DOT), dimethylformamide (DMF), salicylic acid (SA), ammonia water (25.0 ∼ 28.0%), acetic acid, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade, and all aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18 MΩ/cm).

The physical morphologies of nanomaterials were carried out using JEM-1230 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), SU-8010 Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and Aztec X-Max 80 Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) (Oxford Instruments, Oxford, UK). The particle size and potential were analyzed using the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Analyzer (Malvern, Malvern, UK). X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were available using D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany), Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (ThermoFisher, Massachusetts, USA) and Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (ThermoFisher, Massachusetts, USA), respectively. Fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra were analyzed using F97XP spectrometer (Lengguang technology, Shanghai, China) and Shimadzu UV-1601 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime measurement were taken using the Quantaurus-Tau fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Hamamatsu Photonics, Hamamatsu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silk-based carbon quantum dots (SCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: SCQDs were prepared using silk as carbon source by a facile hydrothermal method (Shi, Lin, Wei, Li, Nie, He, et al., 2021). Specifically, silk fibers were firstly obtained by degumming sericin proteins from silkworm cocoons (Shi, Li, Shi, Zhang, Zhou, Li, et al., 2018). Then, 50 mg of silk fibers was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol followed by the addition of 10 μL of ammonia solution, and a 10 min of sonication at room temperature was performed after stirring for 10 min. Next, the mixed solution was transferred to a sealed reaction kettle and kept at 180 °C for 12 h, to obtain the raw SCQDs. Thereafter, the raw SCQDs were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove non-carbonized silk fibers, and the purified SCQDs were collected, freeze-dried and kept at 4 °C until use.

The synthetic procedure of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs was carried out using the similar strategies as described previously (Shi, et al., 2021; Wang, Xiao, Li, Li, Liu, Zhang, et al., 2018). Briefly, iron(II) acetate anhydrous (347.86 mg, 2.0 mmol), 1.2 mL of SCQDs (0.5 mg/mL) and 800 μL of Cur (1 mg/mL) were dissolved in a mixed solvent of DMF (6.0 mL), ethanol (6.0 mL) and deionized water (6.0 mL) at 1:1:1 vol ratio, followed by 20 min of constant stirring. Next, 39.63 mg of DOT (0.2 mmol) and 60.05 mg of acetic acid (1.0 mmol) was sequentially added into the above mixture under stirring. Then the prepared mixture was transferred to a reaction kettle and heated at 120 °C for 4 h in an oven. After completion of the reaction, the as-prepared products were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and washed with ethanol and deionized water 3 times to remove excess reagents. Finally, the obtained SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs were dried and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

500 μL of the SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs solution (50 μg/mL) was mixed with 500 μL of different concentrations of SA solution. Then 500 μL of fresh distilled water was added to the above mixture and followed by thorough shaking. After 15 min of incubation, fluorescence spectra of the probe were measured and recorded using a F97XP spectrophotometer with excitation at 330 nm. A standard curve was calculated for estimating SA levels based on the ratio of fluorescence intensity of Cur and CQDs at 408 nm and 540 nm (I408/I540) varying with the SA concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SA levels in rice samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three different varieties of rice, one resistant variety “Nanjing 46” and two susceptible varieties “Yongyou 9” and “Jiaheyou 218” respectively, were selected as real samples to evaluate the feasibility of the prepared probe. These rice samples were obtained from the scientific research base of Zhejiang Academy of Agricultural Sciences. The fresh rice were firstly pretreated according to previously described (Shi, et al., 2021). Then the levels of SA in these samples were evaluated using the detection procedure described in section 2.4, and the same samples were determined by the reported HPLC-MS method (Wang, Wang, Wang, & Ding, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of morphology and chemical structures
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, silk-based carbon quantum dots (SCQDs) with excellent fluorescence properties were prepared through a green hydrothermal method. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), natural silk was chosen as the precursor of carbon dots for two reasons: One is cocoon silk generated from Bombyx mori silkworm is easily available, environmentally friendly and biocompatible, the other is silk is composed of a variety of amino acids, which can provide large available active sites for charge transfer. These inherent merits of silk make it a promising candidate as biomaterial for sensing application. Herein, the sericin was firstly removed to obtain silk fibroin via a classic degumming process using sodium bicarbonate, then the fluorescent SCQDs using silk fibroin were obtained through simple hydrothermal synthesis. The morphology of prepared SCQDs was shown in Fig. 1(b). It shows SCQDs were well dispersed, and from the inset for high-resolution TEM image of SCQDs, some SCQDs displayed indistinguishable lattice fingerprints (marked with green circle) while some have obvious lattice fringes (marked with yellow circle), indicating the prepared SCQDs possessed crystalline and amorphous states, which has also been observed in previous study (Feng, Zheng, Dong, Lei, Zhang, Xiao, et al., 2014). Besides, the particle size distribution indicates the prepared SCQDs were main in the range of 5–12 nm with a mean particle diameter of 8.48 nm (Fig. S1(a)).

SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs were synthesized with iron(II) acetate as metal nodes and SCQDs and Cur as dopants. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the composite exhibited polyhedral three-dimensional structure, which is consistent with the classical model of MOF-74 (Wu, Chou, Long, Si, Lo, Tsung, et al., 2019). Fig. S1(b) shows the particle size of the composite was mostly within the range of 0.3–1 μm and the size average was 677 nm. Interestingly, from the enlarged view for SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs (Fig. 1(d)), the composite has internal hollowing property, and this result is also confirmed by TEM image of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs (Fig. 1(e)). Such the hollowed and highly porous structure could increase the contact surface of the probe with the analyte in solution, which may facilitate the interaction between them and further improve the sensitivity of the assay. Next, EDS analysis was used to investigate the elemental composition and distribution of prepared SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs from Fig. 1(d). As shown in Fig. 1(f), the composite was composed of Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N) and Iron (Fe) elements. Further exploration and analysis suggested that these elements were distributed throughout the composite (Fig. 1(g)), and the presence of nitrogen revealed the successful doping of SCQDs.

XRD measurements were performed to study the crystalline structures of prepared materials. SCQDs showed a major diffusive peak at around 26° (Fig. 2 (a)), attributed to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon (Wang, Huang, Sun, Zhang, & Lai, 2017), confirming the crystal feature of SCQDs. Importantly, SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs showed not only the diffraction peaks related to SCQDs and Fe-MOFs, but also the peaks related to Cur at around 13 ∼ 24°, indicating Cur existed in the complex, and the introduction of SCQDs and Cur did not affect the crystal structure of Fe-MOFs. Then, FTIR analysis was carried out to characterize their chemical structures. The results were shown in Fig. 2(b). SCQDs exhibited characteristic peaks related to silk protein at 3415, 1670, 1616 and 1397 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of OH, CO, NH and CH/CN groups, respectively, indicating enriched hydroxyl and amino groups were modified on the surface of SCQDs. Fe-MOFs showed distinct peaks at 533, 819, 1020, 1380 and 1670 cm−1, allocated to the bending or stretching vibration of Fe-O, CH, Fe-OH, CH and CO functional groups, respectively. Comparatively, in addition to the characteristic peaks of SCQDs and Fe-MOFs, some other peaks were also found in the spectra of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs composite, which may be related to the groups from curcumin.

To further confirm the elemental compositions and chemical structure of the prepared SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs, XPS investigation was carried out using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS system. Fig. 2(c) shows the XPS survey spectrum of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs. The result reveals the presence of C, N, O and Fe elements in the composites, which keeps consistent with the observations from EDS. The deconvolution of C1s spectrum (Fig. 2(d)) shows main peaks at 284.7, 285.9, 286.7 and 288.1 eV, matching with the CCC, CN, CO/CN and CN/CO bonds, respectively (Wang, Cai, Biesold-McGee, Huang, Ng, Sun, et al., 2020). In the high resolution of N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(e)), the peaks at 399.3 and 401.2 eV can be ascribed to CN and NH bonds, indicating SCQDs with rich amino were modified on the composites. Interestingly, two higher peaks were also found at 406.0 and 406.9 eV in the spectrum, which may be due to that a large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) from Fe-MOFs with negative charge interacted with prepared SCQDs, and could change the electron distributions on the surface of SCQDs, causing the presence of higher binding energies (Wang, et al., 2020). The deconvolution of O 1s orbital shows three peaks located at 531.3, 532.3 and 535.2 eV, corresponding to Fe-O, CO/CO and OH bonds, respectively (Fig. 2(f)). The existence of Fe-O bonds may originate from Fe-MOF and the coordination bonds between Fe and CO of Cur. The above characterization confirmed the successful synthesis of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs.

The excitation wavelength dependences of photoluminescence (PL) intensity for the SCQDs and Cur were studied. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the PL peak position of SCQDs red-shifted with an increase in excitation wavelength from 300 to 420 nm, and the corresponding contour map (Fig. 3(b)) revealed the optimal excitation wavelength (λex) was 330 nm, at which the highest emission intensity (λem) was reached at 408 nm. These results were similar with previous reported works that the blue emission of SCQDs may be attributed to quantum size effect (Maisuls, Wang, Gutierrez Suburu, Wilde, Daniliuc, Brünink, et al., 2021), and the red-shifted reason of the peak positions may be ascribed to amino and hydroxyl functionalization on the surface of SCQDs in the process of silk carbonization (Sk, Ananthanarayanan, Huang, Lim, & Chen, 2014). Compared to SCQDs, the emission peaks position of Cur almost kept unchanged with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 310 to 420 nm (Fig. S2(a)), and the corresponding contour map suggested the emission intensity was strongest at 540 nm under the excitation of 350 nm (Fig. S2(b)). Considering the fluorescence characteristics of both, especially the constant emission peak position of Cur, the wavelength of 330 nm was taken as the optimal excitation in this study.

The effect of Cur on the luminescent properties of SCQDs was also explored. Firstly, the amount of SCQDs (600 μL, 0.5 mg/mL) remained unchanged, and then different amounts of Cur (1.0 mg/mL) were added to investigate the interaction between them. As shown in Fig. 3(c), with increasing Cur concentration from 0 to 400 μL, the fluorescence intensity of SCQDs at 408 nm decreased gradually, accompanied by the increase in fluorescence intensity of Cur at 540 nm. Generally, fluorescence quenching can be caused by several reasons including fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and dynamic or static quenching. Fig. S3(a) showed the substantial overlap between the emission spectrum of SCQDs and the absorption spectrum of Cur, and the explanation of fluorescence quenching of CQDs has been considered to be IFE mechanism according to previous study (Yan, Zu, Xu, Zhou, Bai, Ma, et al., 2019). When the addition amount of Cur was 400 μL, the fluorescence of SCQDs was almost completely quenched. Thus, the mass ratio of SCQDs to Cur was 3:4, which was selected as the optimal strategy for the preparation of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probes.

The fluorescence property of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe and its response to SA were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 3(d), SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs exhibited a pronounced decrease in PL intensity of Cur at 540 nm compared to SCQDs@Cur. In order to rule out the possibility of removing Cur during centrifugation and washing, the waste liquid after centrifugation and washing of the probe was carefully collected, and its fluorescence signal was detected and compared with that after 3 min of synthesis reaction for the probe. The results showed the residual liquid after elution still exhibited obvious fluorescence signal (Fig. S3(b)), indicating excess and unbound Cur was removed from the probe. Remarkably, the sum of the fluorescence intensity of the solution after elution and the probe at λem = 540 nm was far from reaching the initial emissive intensity of Cur, suggesting the fluorescence of Cur in the probe was greatly quenched. This can be explained by the following fact: Cur combined with Fe2+ to form a binary complex in the process of synthesizing the probe, and it has previously been suggested that metal ions can incorporate 1,3-diketones of Cur and cause fluorescence quenching of Cur (Kumar et al., 2021). Nevertheless, it is interesting that when SA was added into SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs system, the fluorescence signal at λem = 408 nm, originating from SCQDs, exhibited significantly enhanced emission peak meanwhile that of Cur at λem = 540 nm showed only slight increase in PL intensity. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the interaction mechanism between the SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe and SA.

UV–Vis absorption and Zeta potential analyses were further carried out to explain the observed phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 3(e), SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs exhibited wide absorption peak at 350–500 nm, which was similar to that of SCQDs@Cur. This indicated the introduction of Fe-MOFs and the combination of Cur with Fe2+ could not affect the absorption properties of Cur. To learn the quenching mechanism of SCQDs by Cur in the SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe, Zeta potentials of prepared materials were determined and the results were shown in Fig. 3(f). SCQDs displayed a negative charge due to the amino group of silk backbone while both Cur and SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs had positive charges, indicating SCQDs could interact with Cur by electrostatic attraction in the probe, and FRET or IFE behavior might occur in the system according to explanation theories of fluorescence quenching. For comparison, the fluorescence lifetime of the probe before and after the introduction of Cur was measured, and the results were shown in Fig. S4. The calculated fluorescence lifetimes were 4.612 and 4.426 ns for SCQDs@Fe-MOFs and SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs, respectively. The almost unchanged fluorescence lifetime revealed the fluorescence quenching of SCQDs was static quenching, and this can be ascribed to IFE behavior because FRET is usually considered as a dynamic quenching process, indicating the introduction of Fe-MOFs did not change the quenching mode for Cur-SCQDs system. Interestingly, when SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe was exposed to SA, the absorption peak at around 410 nm was significantly reduced from Fig. 3(e), which could be attributed to the fact that SA interacted with Cur-Fe2+ to form Cur-Fe2+-SA ternary complex. This changed the absorption properties of Cur at 410 nm, and further inhibited the IFE behavior between Cur and SCQDs and the fluorescence of SCQDs recovered accordingly.

This work further explored the binding mechanism between curcumin and iron ions from the perspective of molecular simulation by using density functional theory calculation (Shi, et al., 2022) and macromolecular docking technology (Yapar, Demir, Kiraz, Özkat, & Yıldız, 2022). Fig. 4 (a) shows the calculated infrared spectra of Cur and Cur@Fe2+ molecules. Obviously, Cur@Fe2+ has an absorption peak at around 540 cm−1, which is consistent with the actual measured FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2(b), indicating the constructed molecular structure and calculation results are reliable. The calculated UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cur@Fe2+ shows the structure has a broad absorption peak in the range of 300–500 nm (Fig. 4(b)), which is beneficial for its energy transfer to SCQDs. Interestingly, after Cur complexed with Fe2+ ion, their energy band gap did not change significantly (Fig. 4(c) and (d)). This may explain that the fluorescence peak position of Cur at 540 nm did not have a red shift or a blue shift. Fig. 4(e) and (f) showed the calculated electrostatic potential distribution of Cur and Cur@Fe2+ structures. Compared to Cur, surface electrons of Cur@Fe2+ leant towards the vicinity of Fe2+ ions, indicating that the introduction of Fe2+ ions altered the charge distribution of Cur. Then molecular docking technique was used to investigate the affinity of K+ ions in plants and Fe2+ ions used in this work for Cur. The results revealed the binding energies of Cur with K+ and Fe2+ ions were −3.4 and −9.6 kcal/mol (Fig. S5(a) and (b)). Generally, binding energies below −5 kcal/mol are considered as stable docking results (Mishra & Dey, 2019). The above results indicated Cur had great affinity with Fe2+ ion. Based on these observations and the above principle, the prepared probe can be used for sensitive detection of SA, and the relevant mechanism representation was depicted in Scheme 1.

Several important factors including amount of iron(Ⅱ) acetate, pH and incubation time were optimized to achieve the best analytical performance of the probe. The fluorescence intensity of Cur at λem = 540 nm was used as the monitoring index to evaluate the quenching property of Fe2+ on Cur. As shown in Fig. S6(a), the response value decreased and then planarized with higher iron(Ⅱ) acetate content applied. This indicated Fe2+ ions were not only as the precursor for the synthesis of Fe-MOFs, but partly combined with Cur and further greatly quenched the fluorescence of Cur. When the amount of introduced Fe2+ ions was 2.0 mmol, the fluorescence of Cur was almost no longer reduced, suggesting excessive Fe2+ ions were not helpful for further quenching its fluorescence. Therefore, 2.0 mmol of iron(Ⅱ) acetate was selected to prepare SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe.

For the optimization of pH and incubation time, 10 μmol/L of SA was used as the analysis object and the ratio of fluorescence intensity of SCQDs to Cur (I408/I540) was as the monitored response signal. As shown in Fig. S6(b), the response value presented obvious pH dependence and it reached the maximum when the probe was in neutral or weak alkaline condition, indicating mild environment may be more conducive to the fluorescence recovery of SCQDs. Thus, pH 7.5 was selected for further optimization. Fig. S6(c) showed the effect of incubation time on response value of the probe. As the incubation time was prolonged, the response value first increased and then reached a dynamic balance after around 15 min of reaction, revealing 15 min of incubation time was sufficient for finish the determination of SA. According to the above results, pH 7.5 and 15 min of incubation time were optimal for this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances of SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe to SA
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optimized protocol, the proposed probe was used for evaluating its sensitivity to SA by recording the emission spectra when exposed to SA with different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), with the increase in the SA concentration from 0 to 200 μmol/L, the emission intensity of SCQDs at λem = 408 nm distinctly increased accompanying with the slightly increased emission intensity of Cur at λem = 540 nm. This can be ascribed to the following fact: addition of SA attracted the attention of Cur-Fe2+ binary complex, and further interacted with each other to form Cur-Fe2+-SA ternary complex. This interaction blocked the IFE behavior between SCQDs and Cur-Fe2+ binary complex, and led to the fluorescence recovery of inhibited SCQDs. Fig. 5(b) showed the response value (I408/I540) of the probe varied stepwise with the concentration of SA in the range of 0–200 μmol/L, and two linear regression equations can be obtained as follows: Y = 0.162X + 0.332 (R2 = 0.986) and Y = 0.011X + 1.546 (R2 = 0.988) for determination of SA concentration ranging from 0.5 to 8 μmol/L and 8–200 μmol/L, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) concentration was calculated to be 0.14 μmol/L on the basis of triple Signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3). A more detailed comparison of the sensitivity and linear range of this work with those of others was presented in Table S1. The comparison shows that the sensing performance of the proposed strategy is comparable or better than those obtained from reported methods. More importantly, the prepared probe has the advantages of lower cost, simple preparation, environmental friendliness and being compatible with large-scale production compared to other works.

Selectivity and stability are also key indicators for practical applications. In this work, 10 μmol/L of SA and 100 μmol/L of other potential interferents (Fe3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Cl-, NO3 –, benzoic acid (BA), methyl salicylate (MeSA), jasmonic acid (JA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and abscisic acid (ABA) were employed for checking the analytical specificity of the probe to SA. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the response value (I408/I540) of the probe was remarkably increased when SA was added into the system. However, no significant changes for that were observed when exposed to the metal ions and anions even at a 10-folds higher concentration than SA, indicating the introduction of Fe-MOFs avoided the coupling effect between Cur and other metal ions, and thus enhanced the anti-interference ability of the probe. Notably, the probe showed a certain response to the structural analogues of SA (e.g. BA and MeSate), revealing there is scope for further improving the specificity of the probe. Nevertheless, the probe can still be used for quantification of SA due to the limited influence of interferents on the output results. Further, the stability of as-prepared probe was evaluated by measuring the response value after 2 weeks. The results showed that the emission intensity of SCQDs decreased slightly after 5 days (Fig. 5(d)), which may be related to the oxidation of the surface structure of SCQDs. Meanwhile, a minor decay of 2.6% for the response value (I408/I540) of the probe could be observed after 15 days of storage, indicating the prepared probe has good long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, rice were used to evaluate the practicality of the developed SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs probe. Three types of rice from one disease-resistant (Nanjing 46) and two infected varieties (Yongyou 9 and Jiaheyou 218) were employed as detection samples, and then the contents of salicylic acid in the above samples were measured using the established fluorescence method and HPLC-MS method, respectively. The obtained results were shown in Table 1 . The salicylic acid content in the disease-resistant rice detected by the prepared probe was 392.9 ng/g, while that in the infected varieties (Yongyou 9 and Jiaheyou 218) were 291.4 and 253.1 ng/g, respectively. The salicylic acid content in disease-resistant rice detected by HPLC-MS method was 380.8 ng/g, and the salicylic acid content in the infected rice (Yongyou 9 and Jiaheyou 218) was 300.7 and 248.8 ng/g, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD, N = 6) of fluorescence method was less than 2.58%. The paired T-test results showed the P values were higher than 0.05, indicating that there was no significant difference between the two methods. From the data analysis, salicylic acid content of rice resistant and infected varieties were both up regulated. This may be because the infected varieties also significantly up regulated the salicylic acid level through gene expression to resist the stress of rice disease and maintain plant related physiological and biochemical activities. The above results indicate that the developed method have the capacity to determine SA levels in biological samples with reliable results.

In this work, a simple ratiometric fluorescence probe was developed for SA detection based on SCQDs@Cur@Fe-MOFs. SCQDs, using silk as raw material, were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, and a self-assembled IFE system was constructed in Fe-MOFs employing the unique properties of Cur and SCQDs. Notably, Fe-MOFs not only plays important role in avoiding self-aggregation of SCQDs, but acts as a bridge for the interaction between Cur and salicylic acid. Specifically, the Fe2+ ions in Fe-MOFs interacted with curcumin and salicylic acid to form Cur-Fe2+-SA ternary complex. The intriguing subtlety for the strategy lies in the use of Fe2+ intermediary from Fe-MOFs to capture SA while avoiding the interference of other metal ions on the detection results. Moreover, the formation of Cur-Fe2+-SA complex changed the absorption properties of curcumin, and further inhibited the IFE behavior between curcumin and SCQDs. Based on the observations, the proposed method realized quantitative detection of SA in the range of 0.5 to 200 μmol/L with the LOD of 0.14 μmol/L. The study on selectivity revealed that the probe exhibited minimal reactivity towards interfering substances, with the exception of certain highly concentrated structural analogues. This finding suggests the probe remains viable for use even in stringent environmental conditions. The satisfactory recoveries of SA in rice confirmed the practical potential of the ratiometric probe for the sensitive detection of SA in complex biological samples. Overall, the present results offer a new insight into the green strategy for SA detection.

Yongqiang Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Wenting Li: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xuetao Hu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xinai Zhang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiaowei Huang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhihua Li: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiaodong Zhai: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Tingting Shen: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jiyong Shi: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yong He: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (32071895). Project of Agricultural Equipment Department of Jiangsu University (NZXB20200212).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137260.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, W., Zhong, H., Zhao, P., Shen, A., Li, H., & Liu, X. (2022). Carbon quantum dot fluorescent probes for food safety detection: Progress, opportunities and challenges. Food Control, 133, 108591.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zheng et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative determination of carbasalate calcium derived metabolites, acetylsalicylic acid and salicylic acid, in six animal foods using liquid-liquid extraction method coupled with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Food Chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, W., Yoo, K.-H., Abd El-Aty, A. M., Park, D.-H., Choi, J.-M., Kim, S.-K., Kang, Y.-S., Zhang, H., Hacımüftüoğlu, A., Bekhit, A. E.-D., Wang, J., Shim, J.-H., & Shin, H.-C. (2019). Quantitative determination of carbasalate calcium derived metabolites, acetylsalicylic acid and salicylic acid, in six animal foods using liquid-liquid extraction method coupled with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Food Chemistry, 278, 744-750.
